frequency.label: forever
frequency.coverageNote: Pre-1700 one-word values are visually tempting but likely mix OCR, spelling normalization, and metadata noise. Use as audit data, not the default public line.

frequency.label: for ever
frequency.coverageNote: The spaced form has substantial pre-1700 signal, but 1500s values should still be treated as early-print/noisy.

frequency.label: forevermore
frequency.coverageNote: Pre-1700 signal is sparse and conflicts with some lexical-source dating. Use for audit only until verified against early-print text.

frequency.label: forever and ever
frequency.coverageNote: Pre-1700 phrase signal is sparse. Use the full series for audit, but public context needs separate attestation or early-print evidence.

coverage.note: This layer stores lexical-source and source-audit records only. It is not a comparable pre-1700 corpus yet.

records.caveat: This is a lexical-source date for the spaced form, not a corpus hit in the project data.
Wiktionary gives the Middle English spaced form before the one-word spelling. This is not yet tied to a project-owned quotation.
Use this as a spelling-history note only until a dated early-print occurrence is verified.
This supports the spaced biblical/liturgical formula. It does not verify the one-word phrase spelling 'forever and ever' before 1700.
Public lexical sources conflict: Merriam-Webster gives 14th century for forevermore, while Etymonline gives 1819 for the emphatic form. Needs OED or early-print verification.
This conflicts with Etymonline's 1819 date. Do not present a first-use claim until checked against OED or source quotations.

investigatedSources.name: EEBO-TCP
investigatedSources.note: Public TCP texts are available, including bulk downloads, but ingesting/searching the corpus responsibly is larger than this data-foundation pass.

investigatedSources.name: EarlyPrint / EarlyPrint Lab
investigatedSources.note: Search and lab tools are suitable for early-print context; needs a dedicated ingestion/search workflow before it can drive visuals.

investigatedSources.name: Google Books Ngram pre-1700
investigatedSources.note: Useful for testing visible frequency signal, but not enough for context or first-use claims.

investigatedSources.name: Oxford English Dictionary
investigatedSources.note: Best source for earliest attested usage and sense history, but subscription access and quotation reuse need a separate workflow.
